sections.content:

Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking the white square in the header selects white color and changes the diamond to white! The white bar is a cursor under the selected color. Now I have: white diamond, purple rect, white rect. Need to use this color selection mechanic.
- Plan: Try arrow keys and SPACE to discover movement/interaction mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
DOWN still doesn't move the diamond. So the diamond only moves diagonally with RIGHT/LEFT.
Let me reconsider the mechanics:
- RIGHT moves the diamond diagonally up-right
- LEFT moves it diagonally down-left
- UP/DOWN don't move it
- Clicking header color squares changes the diamond's color
- SPACE converts white rectangles to purple
Current state:
- White diamond at [[25, 26], [25, 37], [31, 37], [31, 26]]
- Purple rect at [[34, 27], [34, 36], [38, 36], [38, 27]]
- White rect at [[39, 27], [39, 36], [43, 36], [43, 27]]
The template shows white above purple. The center objects are purple above white.
Maybe I need to:
1. Select purple color (click purple square)
2. Move the diamond to overlap with the white rectangle (bottom one)
3. This would paint the white rectangle purple
4. Then I'd have: white diamond, purple rect, purple rect - still not matching
Or maybe:
1. Select white color (already selected)
2. Move the diamond to overlap with the purple rectangle (middle one)
3. This would paint the purple rectangle white
4. Then I'd have: white diamond, white rect, white rect - still not matching
Hmm, but the diamond only moves diagonally with RIGHT/LEFT. RIGHT moves it up-right, LEFT moves it down-left. Currently it's at [[25, 26], [25, 37], [31, 37], [31, 26]]. The purple rect is at [[34, 27], [34, 36], [38, 36], [38, 27]]. The white rect is at [[39, 27], [39, 36], [43, 36], [43, 27]].
To move the diamond down to overlap with the purple rect, I'd need LEFT (which moves down-left). But the diamond is already at the leftmost position (col 26). LEFT would move it further left and down.
Actually, wait. The diamond was originally at [[25, 26], [25, 37], [31, 37], [31, 26]]. After RIGHT, it moved to [[23, 39], [31, 47], [35, 43], [35, 42], [28, 35], [27, 35]]. After LEFT, it came back to [[25, 26], [25, 37], [31, 37], [31, 26]].
So RIGHT moves up-right and LEFT moves down-left. To move the diamond down, I need LEFT. But LEFT moves it both down AND left. The diamond is currently at col 26-37. If I move LEFT, it goes to col 15-26 (approximately), which is further left.
Actually, let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal isn't to paint the rectangles. Maybe the goal is to match the template by arranging the objects. The template has white above purple. Currently the center objects are purple above white.
What if I need to:
1. Select purple color
2. Move the diamond to overlap with the purple rect (they merge into one larger purple object)
3. Then the arrangement is: (merged purple), white - which is purple above white, still not matching
Or what if I need to:
1. Select white color (already selected)
2. Move the diamond to overlap with the purple rect
3. The purple rect becomes white
4. Now I have: white diamond, white rect, white rect
5. Then select purple and paint the bottom white rect purple
6. Now: white diamond, white rect, purple rect - that's white above purple!
But the diamond only moves diagonally with RIGHT/LEFT. Let me figure out how to move it to the purple rect position.
The diamond is at rows 25-31, cols 26-37. The purple rect is at rows 34-38, cols 27-36. To overlap, I need to move the diamond down. LEFT moves down-left.
Let me try LEFT to see where it goes.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: DOWN doesn't move diamond. Diamond only moves diagonally (RIGHT=up-right, LEFT=down-left). Clicking header colors changes diamond color. Current: white diamond, purple rect, white rect. Need to figure out how to arrange colors to match template (white above purple).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try LEFT to move the diamond down-left and see if it can overlap with the purple rect
print("Trying LEFT to move diamond down-left...")
result = action(['LEFT'])
print("Result:", last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed'), last_action_result.get('no_impact'))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Main objects after LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 20 and n['color'] in ['W', 'p']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Let me also try RIGHT to see the pattern
print("\nTrying RIGHT...")
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print("Result:", last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed'), last_action_result.get('no_impact'))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Main objects after RIGHT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 20 and n['color'] in ['W', 'p']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
